naturalMeaning: Almost never brought from home in Brazil's big beach cities — you rent one on the spot from an ambulante or a fixed rental point.

examples.text: Quanto custa a cadeira de praia?
examples.translation: How much is the beach chair?

naturalMeaning: Usually rented together with a cadeira de praia, as a set.

examples.text: Aluga cadeira e guarda-sol ali no quiosque.
examples.translation: You rent a chair and umbrella right there at the kiosk.

translation: to plant/set up the beach umbrella

examples.text: Vamos fincar o guarda-sol mais longe da água.
examples.translation: Let's set up the umbrella farther from the water.

naturalMeaning: A pop-up tent some families bring for babies and small kids — less common than a rented guarda-sol among adults.
A large printed rectangle of light fabric — spread on the sand to lie on, tied as a cover-up, or used as a towel. A beach-bag essential.

examples.text: Estende a canga aqui na sombra.
examples.translation: Spread the canga here in the shade.

naturalMeaning: Used more for drying off after a swim; for lying on the sand, a canga is more typical, especially in Rio.
Technically a brand name (like 'Styrofoam'), but it's the everyday word for any foam cooler — beachgoers bring their own full of drinks, or ambulantes carry theirs full of stock.

examples.text: Traz o isopor com gelo, que a cerveja acaba rápido.
examples.translation: Bring the cooler with ice, the beer runs out fast.

examples.text: Passa protetor solar antes de entrar na água.
examples.translation: Put on sunscreen before going in the water.

naturalMeaning: Often just shortened to 'chinelo'. Havaianas is the classic Brazilian brand, worn everywhere near the coast.
The standard men's swimwear on Brazilian beaches — snug-fitting, not baggy board shorts. Considered completely normal, not risqué.

examples.text: Ele comprou uma sunga nova pra viagem.
examples.translation: He bought new swim trunks for the trip.

examples.text: Comprei um biquíni novo pra praia.
examples.translation: I bought a new bikini for the beach.

naturalMeaning: Walks the sand all day selling food, drinks, and gear, usually announcing what they're carrying with a shouted, sing-song call. See the culture notes for how buying from one actually works.

examples.text: O ambulante passa aqui de meia em meia hora.
examples.translation: The vendor comes by here every half hour.

naturalMeaning: Usually served straight from the green coconut with a straw, chilled in an ambulante's cooler or at a quiosque.

examples.text: Água de coco gelada, por favor!
examples.translation: A cold coconut water, please!

naturalMeaning: Short for 'chá mate' — sold ice-cold by ambulantes, classically shouted together with 'água de coco' as a pair.

examples.text: Mate e limonada, geladinho!
examples.translation: Iced mate and lemonade, ice cold!

naturalMeaning: A light, crunchy manioc-starch ring, practically a symbol of Rio's beaches — sold in bags by ambulantes, often with a side of requeijão cream cheese for dipping.

examples.text: Um biscoito Globo com requeijão, por favor.
examples.translation: A Globo biscuit with cream cheese, please.

naturalMeaning: Grilled on a small portable charcoal stove the vendor carries on their head or a cart, then handed over on a skewer, sometimes with oregano or molasses drizzle.
A fixed stall right on the sand or boardwalk selling drinks, snacks, and often renting chairs — many have their own name and are used as meeting points, like 'me acha no quiosque do Zé'.

examples.text: Te espero no quiosque, do lado da barraca de açaí.
examples.translation: I'll wait for you at the kiosk, next to the açaí stand.

naturalMeaning: The chanted, almost musical shout ambulantes use to advertise what they're carrying without stopping — e.g. 'Ó o mate, ó a água de coco!' — recognizable even before you see the person.

examples.text: Dá pra reconhecer o ambulante só pelo pregão.
examples.translation: You can recognize the vendor just by his call.

naturalMeaning: Planted by lifeguards near their posto and changed through the day depending on sea conditions — check it before you go in.

examples.text: Olha a bandeira antes de entrar no mar.
examples.translation: Check the flag before going into the sea.

naturalMeaning: Means the sea is dangerous — strong currents or ressaca. Wading at the edge is usually tolerated, but swimming out is not.

examples.text: Hoje tá bandeira vermelha, melhor não nadar.
examples.translation: It's a red flag today, better not to swim.

naturalMeaning: Sea conditions are moderate — okay to swim, but stay closer in and keep an eye on the current.

examples.text: Bandeira verde hoje, pode nadar sossegado.
examples.translation: Green flag today, you can swim without worry.

naturalMeaning: Also means 'hangover' in other contexts — here it refers to a strong undertow pulling swimmers out, the main reason for a red flag.
Stationed at each numbered posto; identifiable by red-and-yellow trunks and the raised flag beside their tower.

examples.text: Pergunta pro guarda-vidas se pode nadar.
examples.translation: Ask the lifeguard if it's okay to swim.

examples.text: Na maré alta a faixa de areia fica bem menor.
examples.translation: At high tide the strip of sand gets a lot smaller.

naturalMeaning: Volleyball rules, but no hands or arms allowed — only feet, chest, and head. Invented on Rio's beaches in the 1960s.

examples.text: Todo fim de tarde eles jogam futevôlei na quadra.
examples.translation: Every late afternoon they play footvolley on the court.

naturalMeaning: A loose, no-rules circle where players just keep the ball in the air with their feet or head — an easygoing way to join in without knowing futevôlei's rules.

examples.text: Bora bater um altinho enquanto o sol não esquenta.
examples.translation: Let's kick around an altinho while the sun's not too hot yet.

naturalMeaning: Played with wooden paddles and a rubber ball, just keeping a rally going — no net, no score, popular right at the water's edge.
The full-net, official-rules version, as opposed to the looser altinho — Brazil is a powerhouse in this sport internationally.
In Rio, the numbered lifeguard towers along Copacabana and Ipanema double as the city's real navigational landmarks — locals give directions and meeting points by posto number, not street names.

examples.text: A gente sempre para no mesmo posto.
examples.translation: We always stop at the same posto.

translation: Posto 9 (a famous stretch of Ipanema beach)
naturalMeaning: The best-known posto in Ipanema, historically associated with young, artsy, and LGBTQ+-friendly beachgoers — mentioning it to a Carioca instantly signals a specific crowd and vibe, not just a location.

examples.text: Vamos marcar no Posto 9?
examples.translation: Shall we meet at Posto 9?

naturalMeaning: The wide, wave-patterned mosaic sidewalk running along famous beaches like Copacabana — used for walking, running, cycling, and as a reference line for directions.

examples.text: Encontro vocês no calçadão em frente ao quiosque.
examples.translation: I'll meet you all on the boardwalk in front of the kiosk.

examples.text: A areia tá bem quente hoje, leva o chinelo.
examples.translation: The sand is really hot today, bring your flip-flops.

naturalMeaning: What you're really paying for when you rent a guarda-sol — Brazilians take having sombra on a long beach day seriously.

examples.text: Prefiro ficar na sombra, tá um sol forte.
examples.translation: I'd rather stay in the shade, the sun's strong.

translation: to go hang out at the beach
naturalMeaning: Not just 'to go swimming' — implies the whole ritual of spending the day there: chair, umbrella, friends, snacks. A core everyday plan, not a special trip.

examples.text: Domingo a gente vai pegar praia cedo.
examples.translation: On Sunday we're going to hit the beach early.

examples.text: Vou só pegar um solzinho antes de ir embora.
examples.translation: I'm just going to catch a little sun before I leave.

translation: to take a dip / go for a swim

examples.text: Tá calor, vou dar um mergulho rapidinho.
examples.translation: It's hot, I'm going to take a quick dip.

naturalMeaning: The standard way to ask a beach neighbor or friend to keep an eye on your stuff while you swim — see the culture notes on leaving valuables.

examples.text: Pode tomar conta das minhas coisas um minuto?
examples.translation: Can you watch my stuff for a minute?

examples.text: Domingo de sol, a praia tá sempre lotada.
examples.translation: On a sunny Sunday, the beach is always packed.

naturalMeaning: Also slang for an inflated body shape ('tá com uma boinha') — but at the beach it just means an inflatable ring or float, usually for kids.
